summary: Configure AWS Secrets Manager for Environment-Specific Service Credentials
description: Establish a complete secrets management foundation for LoanFlow 360 using AWS Secrets Manager. This epic covers provisioning all environment-namespaced secrets for database passwords, JWT signing secret, Redis auth token, MSK SASL credentials, and DocumentDB password across dev and production environments; updating every Spring Boot service to consume credentials exclusively from environment variables injected at ECS task startup; configuring automatic secret rotation for RDS database passwords; and auditing the repository and Docker image layers for any credential exposure. The output is a system where no plaintext credential exists in source control, application config files, or built container images — all sensitive values are resolved at runtime from Secrets Manager through ECS secrets injection.
functionality: provision and manage all LoanFlow 360 service credentials in AWS Secrets Manager with environment isolation, automatic rotation, and runtime injection into ECS tasks — with no credentials stored in source control or container images
benefit: eliminate credential sprawl and exposure risk across dev and production environments, meet least-privilege and secret rotation compliance requirements, and ensure every service reads the correct credentials for its environment without configuration changes to the deployed artifact

stories.summary: Provision Secrets Manager database credential secrets for all transactional services
stories.description:
Create AWS Secrets Manager secrets for the RDS MySQL database passwords used by the four transactional LoanFlow 360 services across both environments. Secrets follow a consistent namespace: /loanflow/{env}/{service-name}/db-password. The DocumentDB password for notification-service is provisioned under the same pattern. A separate secret stores the master RDS admin password used for provisioning and rotation. All secrets are created with the SecretString format and tagged for cost allocation and access auditing.

Secrets to provision:
- /loanflow/dev/identity-service/db-password
- /loanflow/prod/identity-service/db-password
- /loanflow/dev/loan-service/db-password
- /loanflow/prod/loan-service/db-password
- /loanflow/dev/document-service/db-password
- /loanflow/prod/document-service/db-password
- /loanflow/dev/underwriting-service/db-password
- /loanflow/prod/underwriting-service/db-password
- /loanflow/dev/notification-service/docdb-password
- /loanflow/prod/notification-service/docdb-password

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: All ten database credential secrets exist in Secrets Manager
acceptanceCriteria.when: aws secretsmanager list-secrets --filters Key=name,Values=/loanflow is run
acceptanceCriteria.then: all ten secrets are listed with the correct /loanflow/{env}/{service-name}/ path and no secret is missing for any environment-service combination

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Secret values are strong randomly generated passwords
acceptanceCriteria.given: a secret is retrieved via aws secretsmanager get-secret-value
acceptanceCriteria.then: the password is at least 32 characters, contains uppercase, lowercase, digits, and special characters, and was not set to a default or placeholder value

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Secrets are tagged for environment and service ownership
acceptanceCriteria.given: a secret is described via the AWS CLI
acceptanceCriteria.then: each secret carries tags Environment={env}, Service={service-name}, and Project=loanflow-360

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Dev and production secrets are isolated — dev secret value does not appear in prod
acceptanceCriteria.given: both dev and prod secrets exist for the same service
acceptanceCriteria.when: both secret values are retrieved and compared
acceptanceCriteria.then: the password values are different, confirming environment isolation

tasks.summary: Define the /loanflow/{env}/{service}/ secret naming convention and document it
tasks.description: Document the full secret path convention in <infra/secrets/README.md>: sensitive credentials live at /loanflow/{env}/{service-name}/{credential-type} (e.g. /loanflow/prod/loan-service/db-password). Non-sensitive config lives in Parameter Store at the same path pattern. This distinction governs which ECS task definition field — secrets (Secrets Manager) versus environment valueFrom (Parameter Store) — each value uses.

tasks.summary: Provision database password secrets for identity-service and loan-service (dev and prod)
tasks.description: Use aws secretsmanager create-secret to provision /loanflow/dev/identity-service/db-password, /loanflow/prod/identity-service/db-password, /loanflow/dev/loan-service/db-password, and /loanflow/prod/loan-service/db-password. Generate 32-character passwords using aws secretsmanager get-random-password --password-length 32 --exclude-punctuation false. Tag each secret with Environment, Service, and Project tags.

tasks.summary: Provision database password secrets for document-service and underwriting-service (dev and prod)
tasks.description: Provision /loanflow/dev/document-service/db-password, /loanflow/prod/document-service/db-password, /loanflow/dev/underwriting-service/db-password, and /loanflow/prod/underwriting-service/db-password following the same generation and tagging steps. Update each corresponding RDS instance master password to match the provisioned secret value using the AWS console or RDS API.

tasks.summary: Provision DocumentDB password secret for notification-service (dev and prod)
tasks.description: Provision /loanflow/dev/notification-service/docdb-password and /loanflow/prod/notification-service/docdb-password. Update the DocumentDB cluster master password to match. The notification-service uses these credentials to authenticate to DocumentDB using the MongoDB-compatible driver; the secret value must not contain characters that require URL-encoding in a MongoDB connection string (avoid @, /, ?, #, %, =, &).

stories.summary: Provision shared infrastructure credential secrets across environments
stories.description:
Create Secrets Manager secrets for the credentials shared across multiple services or tied to infrastructure components rather than a single service database. This includes the JWT signing secret used by the API Gateway and identity-service, the Redis auth token used by the API Gateway and loan-service for rate limiting and caching, and the MSK SASL/SCRAM credentials used by all Kafka-producing and consuming services. Each secret is provisioned for both dev and production under the /loanflow/{env}/ namespace.

Secrets to provision:
- /loanflow/dev/jwt-secret
- /loanflow/prod/jwt-secret
- /loanflow/dev/redis-auth-token
- /loanflow/prod/redis-auth-token
- /loanflow/dev/msk-sasl-credentials (JSON: username, password)
- /loanflow/prod/msk-sasl-credentials (JSON: username, password)

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: JWT secret is consistent across all services that consume it within an environment
acceptanceCriteria.given: the api-gateway and identity-service both read /loanflow/{env}/jwt-secret
acceptanceCriteria.when: a JWT signed by identity-service is validated by api-gateway
acceptanceCriteria.then: the signature verification succeeds because both services resolved the same secret value from Secrets Manager

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: MSK SASL credentials secret contains both username and password as a JSON object
acceptanceCriteria.when: the SecretString is parsed as JSON
acceptanceCriteria.then: the object contains keys username and password, both non-empty, matching the SASL/SCRAM user registered on the MSK cluster

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Redis auth token secret value matches the ElastiCache cluster auth token
acceptanceCriteria.when: the value is used as the AUTH token in a Redis connection attempt to the ElastiCache cluster
acceptanceCriteria.then: the AUTH command succeeds and the connection is established

tasks.summary: Provision JWT signing secret for dev and production
tasks.description: Generate a 64-character cryptographically random string for each environment using aws secretsmanager get-random-password --password-length 64 --exclude-punctuation true. Store as /loanflow/dev/jwt-secret and /loanflow/prod/jwt-secret with a plain SecretString value (not JSON-wrapped). Tag with Environment, Project, and UsedBy=api-gateway,identity-service.

tasks.summary: Provision Redis auth token secret and sync with ElastiCache cluster
tasks.description: Generate a 32-character alphanumeric token for each environment (ElastiCache requires the auth token to be alphanumeric only). Store as /loanflow/dev/redis-auth-token and /loanflow/prod/redis-auth-token. Confirm the token matches the auth-token value configured on the ElastiCache cluster during infrastructure provisioning. If the cluster was provisioned with a different token, update the cluster auth token via the ElastiCache modify-replication-group API.

tasks.summary: Provision MSK SASL credentials secret as a JSON object for dev and production
tasks.description:
Create a SASL/SCRAM user on the MSK cluster for each environment. Store the credentials as /loanflow/dev/msk-sasl-credentials and /loanflow/prod/msk-sasl-credentials with SecretString value:
```
{"username": "loanflow-dev", "password": "<generated>"}
```
Associate the secret with the MSK cluster using aws kafka batch-associate-scram-secret so MSK uses it directly for SCRAM authentication.

stories.summary: Update Spring Boot service configuration to consume all credentials from environment variables
stories.description:
Remove all hardcoded credentials and connection strings from every Spring Boot service's application.yaml and application-dev.yaml files. Replace each value with a ${ENV_VAR} Spring property placeholder that ECS secrets injection populates at task startup. The six services each have their own set of required environment variables derived from their backing service dependencies. Local development remains functional by providing safe localhost defaults in the placeholder syntax: ${ENV_VAR:localhost-default}. No service should be able to start in ECS without all required secrets being resolved.

Services in scope: api-gateway, identity-service, loan-service, document-service, underwriting-service, notification-service

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: No hardcoded credentials remain in any application.yaml or application-dev.yaml
acceptanceCriteria.given: all six service config files are audited
acceptanceCriteria.when: a grep search runs across all application.yaml files for common credential patterns
acceptanceCriteria.then: no matches are found for patterns such as password:, secret:, token:, or jdbc:// containing a literal credential string; all such values use ${ENV_VAR} syntax

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Service fails to start in ECS when a required environment variable is absent
acceptanceCriteria.given: a task definition is missing the DB_PASSWORD secret injection for loan-service
acceptanceCriteria.when: the ECS task attempts to start
acceptanceCriteria.then: Spring Boot fails during datasource initialisation with a clear error referencing the unresolved ${DB_PASSWORD} placeholder, and the task transitions to STOPPED

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Service starts successfully for local development without ECS secrets injection
acceptanceCriteria.given: a developer runs the service locally using the dev profile without setting ECS-injected environment variables
acceptanceCriteria.when: spring-boot:run is executed with the dev profile
acceptanceCriteria.then: the service starts using localhost defaults from ${DB_HOST:localhost} and ${DB_PASSWORD:} placeholders without throwing a missing property exception

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: JWT secret is read from an environment variable by both api-gateway and identity-service
acceptanceCriteria.given: JWT_SECRET is injected via ECS secrets from /loanflow/{env}/jwt-secret
acceptanceCriteria.when: identity-service signs a token and api-gateway validates it
acceptanceCriteria.then: token validation succeeds without any hardcoded secret value in either service's config file

tasks.summary: Externalise datasource credentials in identity-service and loan-service application.yaml
tasks.description: In <backend/identity-service/src/main/resources/application.yaml> and <backend/loan-service/src/main/resources/application.yaml>, replace spring.datasource.url, spring.datasource.username, and spring.datasource.password with ${DB_HOST:localhost}, ${DB_USERNAME:root}, and ${DB_PASSWORD:} placeholders. Apply the same change to application-dev.yaml, keeping localhost defaults for local runs.

tasks.summary: Externalise datasource credentials in document-service and underwriting-service application.yaml
tasks.description: Apply the same datasource placeholder pattern to <backend/document-service/src/main/resources/application.yaml> and <backend/underwriting-service/src/main/resources/application.yaml>. These services also use MySQL; the same DB_HOST, DB_USERNAME, DB_PASSWORD environment variable names are used consistently across all four transactional services.

tasks.summary: Externalise Redis, Kafka, and JWT credentials across all services
Externalise DocumentDB connection and S3 bucket name in notification-service and document-service
tasks.description: In <backend/notification-service/src/main/resources/application.yaml>, replace the MongoDB URI with spring.data.mongodb.uri: ${DOCDB_URI:mongodb://localhost:27017/notificationdb}. In <backend/document-service/src/main/resources/application.yaml>, replace the S3 bucket name with ${S3_DOCUMENTS_BUCKET:local-documents-bucket}. Add corresponding environment variable entries to the ECS task definition environment blocks.

tasks.summary: Add application startup validation to fail fast on missing required credentials
tasks.description: For each service, add a @PostConstruct validation bean or rely on Spring Boot's fail-fast property binding by annotating config properties classes with @Validated and @NotBlank on required fields. This ensures a missing DB_PASSWORD or JWT_SECRET causes an immediate startup failure with a clear error message rather than a runtime NullPointerException on first request.

stories.summary: Configure automatic secret rotation for RDS database passwords
stories.description: Enable AWS Secrets Manager automatic rotation for all eight RDS database password secrets (four services × two environments) using the AWS-provided Lambda rotation function for Amazon RDS. Rotation is scheduled every 30 days for production secrets and every 90 days for dev secrets. The rotation function updates both the Secrets Manager secret value and the RDS user password in a single atomic operation, ensuring no service downtime during rotation. ECS tasks pick up the new credential on the next task restart; immediate forced rotation is tested in the dev environment to validate the full rotation-to-restart cycle.

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Rotation updates the secret value and the RDS user password atomically
acceptanceCriteria.when: a manual rotation is triggered via aws secretsmanager rotate-secret
acceptanceCriteria.then: the secret value in Secrets Manager is updated to a new password and the corresponding RDS MySQL user password is changed to match within the same rotation cycle

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: A new ECS task reads the rotated password and connects to RDS successfully
acceptanceCriteria.given: secret rotation has completed for loan-service
acceptanceCriteria.when: a new ECS task starts for loan-service and ECS injects the updated secret value
acceptanceCriteria.then: the Spring Boot datasource connects to RDS using the new password without connection errors

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Rotation schedule is set to 30 days for production secrets
acceptanceCriteria.given: rotation is configured on all four production database password secrets
acceptanceCriteria.when: each secret's rotation configuration is described
acceptanceCriteria.then: the AutomaticallyRotateAfterDays value is 30 for all production secrets and 90 for dev secrets

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Rotation failure triggers a CloudWatch alarm
acceptanceCriteria.given: a CloudWatch alarm monitors the RotationFailed metric for all loanflow secrets
acceptanceCriteria.when: a rotation Lambda function fails to update an RDS password
acceptanceCriteria.then: the alarm transitions to ALARM state and an SNS notification is sent to the operations team

tasks.summary: Enable Secrets Manager rotation using the AWS-provided RDS rotation Lambda
tasks.description: For each of the eight RDS database password secrets, call aws secretsmanager rotate-secret with --rotation-lambda-arn pointing to the aws-secretsmanager-rotation-lambdas SecretsManagerRDSMySQLRotationSingleUser function deployed in the VPC. Set rotation rules with AutomaticallyAfterDays 30 for production secrets and 90 for dev secrets. The Lambda requires access to both the RDS instance and Secrets Manager; confirm the Lambda's VPC and security group configuration allows outbound to the rds-sg and Secrets Manager VPC endpoint.

tasks.summary: Deploy the RDS rotation Lambda into the VPC private subnets
tasks.description: If the AWS-provided rotation Lambda is not already deployed in the account, use the AWS Serverless Application Repository to deploy aws-secretsmanager-rotation-lambdas into the VPC private subnets. Attach a Lambda security group that allows outbound to port 3306 (rds-sg) and HTTPS to the Secrets Manager VPC endpoint. Grant the Lambda execution role secretsmanager:GetSecretValue, secretsmanager:PutSecretValue, and secretsmanager:DescribeSecret.

tasks.summary: Trigger and validate a manual rotation for all dev environment database secrets
tasks.description: After enabling rotation, trigger an immediate rotation for each dev environment database secret using aws secretsmanager rotate-secret --secret-id /loanflow/dev/{service-name}/db-password. Confirm each rotation completes with RotationEnabled true and LastRotatedDate updated. Force a new ECS task deployment for each service in the dev cluster and confirm the task starts successfully with the new credentials.

tasks.summary: Create CloudWatch alarm for Secrets Manager RotationFailed metric
tasks.description: Create a CloudWatch metric alarm on the AWS/SecretsManager namespace for the RotationFailed metric, filtered to secrets whose name starts with /loanflow/. Set the alarm threshold to >= 1 failure over a 5-minute evaluation period. Wire the alarm to an SNS topic that notifies the operations team. This alarm is the primary signal that a rotated credential has not been applied and a service restart may be needed.

stories.summary: Audit source control and Docker image layers for credential exposure
stories.description: Perform a retrospective audit of the LoanFlow 360 git history and all built Docker images to confirm no credentials have been committed or baked into image layers. Install a pre-commit hook using git-secrets to block future credential commits. Add a .gitignore rule to exclude all .env files and local secret files from version control. Scan each Docker image layer history for environment variables or files containing sensitive patterns. Document the audit findings and sign off that the repository and image registry are clean before production deployment.

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Git history scan finds no committed credentials
acceptanceCriteria.given: the git-secrets or truffleHog scan runs against the full repository history
acceptanceCriteria.then: no secrets, passwords, or private keys are detected in any commit across all branches; any findings are remediated before this story is accepted

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Pre-commit hook blocks a commit containing a credential pattern
acceptanceCriteria.given: git-secrets is installed and configured with AWS and common password patterns
acceptanceCriteria.when: a developer attempts to commit a file containing a string matching a credential pattern (e.g. password=secret123)
acceptanceCriteria.then: the commit is rejected with a message identifying the offending pattern and file

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Docker image layer history contains no plaintext credentials
acceptanceCriteria.given: a Docker image has been built and pushed to ECR for any LoanFlow 360 service
acceptanceCriteria.when: docker history --no-trunc is run against the image
acceptanceCriteria.then: no environment variable assignments containing passwords, tokens, or secrets appear in any layer command

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: .env files and local secret files are excluded from version control
acceptanceCriteria.when: a developer creates a .env file in any service directory
acceptanceCriteria.then: git status does not list the .env file as untracked and git add refuses to stage it

tasks.summary: Scan full git history for committed credentials using truffleHog or git-secrets
tasks.description: Run trufflehog git file://. --since-commit HEAD~100 or git-secrets --scan-history against the repository. Review all findings. If any credential is found in history, rotate the affected credential immediately in Secrets Manager and in the backing service, then remove it from git history using git filter-repo or BFG Repo Cleaner. Force-push the cleaned history and notify all contributors to re-clone.

tasks.summary: Install and configure git-secrets pre-commit hook for all contributors
tasks.description: Install git-secrets on the developer workstations (brew install git-secrets on macOS, manual install on Windows). Run git secrets --install in the repository root to install the pre-commit hook. Run git secrets --register-aws to add AWS credential patterns. Add custom patterns for common credential keywords: git secrets --add 'password\s*=\s*\S+' and git secrets --add 'secret\s*=\s*\S+'. Document the setup steps in the repository README under a Security section.

tasks.summary: Update .gitignore to exclude .env files, local secret files, and application-local.yaml
tasks.description:
Add the following entries to the root .gitignore if not already present:
```
.env
.env.*
*.local.yaml
application-local.yaml
application-local.properties
secrets/
*.pem
*.key
```
Verify that existing .env files (if any) are untracked after the update. Add the same patterns to each service's backend directory .gitignore for defence in depth.

tasks.summary: Inspect ECR image layer history for all six services for credential exposure
tasks.description: For each service image currently in ECR, pull the image locally and run docker history --no-trunc <image>. Grep the output for patterns: ENV.*PASSWORD, ENV.*SECRET, ENV.*TOKEN, ENV.*KEY. If any match is found, identify which Dockerfile layer or build step introduced it, remediate the build process, rebuild and push a clean image, and delete the compromised image tag from ECR using aws ecr batch-delete-image.
